Regions/examples: US ESI; NHS England/RCEM; AHRQ ED CAP workflow
Real entry points: walk-in, ambulance/EMS, NHS 111/UTC referral, urgent care escalation
First touch: registration/front desk, streaming clinician, triage RN or RAT clinician
First information collected: chief complaint, appearance, acuity, high-risk state, pain/distress, prior ED/EMS info; in CAP example age/RR/BP/confusion/BUN
Vitals/tests/imaging timing: vitals at or after triage; ED provider orders labs/imaging; radiology/lab return into ED workflow; ESI uses danger-zone vitals after resource prediction
Results/referral/follow-up ownership: ED clinician disposes: discharge, admission, specialist/SDEC, other service; results pending/discharge create ownership risk
If symptoms continue but tests are norma: safety-net discharge instructions, repeat ED return, GP follow-up; real failures show serious disease often found at later return
Signal-loss / black zones: pre-triage occult waits; resource score mistaken for diagnostic confidence; high-risk mimic; pending/abnormal results after discharge

Regions/examples: England UTCs and digital self-service; US urgent-care policies
Real entry points: walk-in, booked appointment, NHS 111 referral, GP/ED redirection
First touch: reception/intake, digital kiosk, nurse/rooming staff, UTC clinician
First information collected: reason for visit, demographics, self-triage answers, red flags; pulse/BP and other observations
Vitals/tests/imaging timing: US urgent care policies aim vitals early/within 15 minutes; UTCs may do ECG, blood tests and X-ray where available
Results/referral/follow-up ownership: onsite treatment, prescription, ED transfer, specialist/GP referral; GP update/post-event message in UTC standards
If symptoms continue but tests are norma: repeat urgent-care visit, GP follow-up, escalation to ED; depends on patient recognizing deterioration
Signal-loss / black zones: non-clinical intake red-flag recognition; digital literacy/language; wrong redirection; lack of diagnostic capability

Regions/examples: England modern GP access/care navigation; NICE NG12; HSSIB continuity; US primary-care diagnostic error literature
Real entry points: phone, online form, walk-in/reception, portal, repeat visit, hospital/ED discharge follow-up
First touch: reception/care navigator, online system, nurse/GP clinical triage
First information collected: reason for contact, symptom history, duration, red flags, prior results/referrals, medication/family/history, patient concern
Vitals/tests/imaging timing: vitals/exam during appointment; GP orders labs/imaging or refers; results return to practice inbox and need review/action
Results/referral/follow-up ownership: GP/nurse/admin team, named clinician, test-results workflow, referral pathway, safety-net plan
If symptoms continue but tests are norma: safety-netting with time-bound review; after 3 presentations use Jess's Rule; persistent symptoms despite negative tests need monitoring/referral
Signal-loss / black zones: administrative routing as triage; normal-test trap; repeat visits across clinicians; EHR not surfacing unresolved symptoms; abnormal results not followed up

Regions/examples: US HRSA health centers; FQHC FIT navigation
Real entry points: scheduled/walk-in primary care, outreach, mailed FIT, hospital/ED discharge, referral from community partners
First touch: front desk, MA/nurse, clinician, care coordinator/navigator
First information collected: demographics, insurance/social needs, reason for visit, vitals, preventive gaps, outside discharge data, screening results
Vitals/tests/imaging timing: labs/vitals in clinic; screening tests such as FIT mailed/returned; colonoscopy referral after abnormal FIT
Results/referral/follow-up ownership: health center must maintain referral/hospital arrangements; navigators track abnormal screening follow-up
If symptoms continue but tests are norma: care coordination/PCMH process; patient navigation addresses barriers and tracks completion
Signal-loss / black zones: outside data not received; abnormal FIT not closed; GI partnership and documentation gaps; safety-net patients must chase care

Regions/examples: Uganda rural OPD; WHO IMCI; WHO PEN
Real entry points: outpatient department first contact, community referral, walk-in, child brought by caregiver
First touch: nurse/OPD triage staff, primary-care health worker, clinician
First information collected: presenting complaint, danger signs, vital signs where available, history/signs/symptoms algorithm; resource/supply constraints
Vitals/tests/imaging timing: formal diagnostics may be limited or absent; algorithms guide management; structured triage can be paper checklist
Results/referral/follow-up ownership: treat onsite, refer severe illness, counsel caregiver; referral/counter-referral often weak
If symptoms continue but tests are norma: return if danger signs/persistence; resource constraints may make follow-up informal
Signal-loss / black zones: no formal triage, stockouts, transport/referral failure, algorithm edge cases, poor documentation

Regions/examples: NHS 111 online; GP online consultation; AHRQ telehealth safety
Real entry points: website/app/phone, online consultation form, video/phone visit, remote monitoring
First touch: patient self-entry, algorithm, admin/care navigator, remote clinician
First information collected: age/sex/postcode, main symptom, structured answers/free text/photos, patient-measured vitals, prior records if available
Vitals/tests/imaging timing: vitals/exam limited to patient devices/visual exam; clinician may convert to in-person, labs/imaging, ED/UTC/GP/pharmacy
Results/referral/follow-up ownership: service disposition or callback; GP practice handles online requests; medical-home integration improves safety
If symptoms continue but tests are norma: convert remote to face-to-face; safety-net advice; monitor persistent symptoms and failed digital access
Signal-loss / black zones: single-symptom algorithms; limited physical exam; digital exclusion; form caps; lost long-term/proactive care

Regions/examples: NHS e-RS Referral Assessment Services; Denmark NSSC diagnostic centres; outpatient advice/guidance
Real entry points: GP/referrer sends referral; advice/guidance request; direct-to-test; triage service
First touch: provider triage clinician/admin; specialist consultant; diagnostic centre
First information collected: referral letter/template, previous tests, symptoms, red flags, reason for referral, GP gut feeling in Denmark
Vitals/tests/imaging timing: specialist may request more info, book appointment, redirect, advise primary care, or send direct to diagnostic test
Results/referral/follow-up ownership: RAS/worklist owner; referrer and patient need visibility; diagnostic centre owns pathway once accepted
If symptoms continue but tests are norma: advice-only outcome needs explicit follow-up and re-referral criteria
Signal-loss / black zones: wrong specialty; worklist/status errors; insufficient referral information; advice without safety net

Regions/examples: NHS radiology follow-up; CDC DxEx; Israel Maccabi diagnostics
Real entry points: referral from GP/ED/inpatient; direct-to-test; hospital follow-up; lab/imaging order
First touch: diagnostic reception/collection site, technician, lab/radiology specialist, requesting clinician
First information collected: referral/order, clinical question, samples/images, result report, critical/unexpected findings
Vitals/tests/imaging timing: tests performed after order; radiology/lab reports return to EHR/inbox; suspicious imaging/pending tests need closure
Results/referral/follow-up ownership: requesting clinician/hospital team/GP; result-owner ambiguity common across institutions
If symptoms continue but tests are norma: repeat imaging/labs or specialist review; if normal but symptoms persist, safety-net owner needed
Signal-loss / black zones: result delivered but not acted on; read receipt not monitored; patient not notified; pending discharge results

Regions/examples: NHS bowel/breast/cervical; CDC navigation; Japan SHC/SHG; South Korea NHIS
Real entry points: invitation, mailed kit, workplace/insurer/national eligibility, outreach/navigation
First touch: programme office, lab, screening centre, navigator, insurer/authorized hospital
First information collected: eligibility, questionnaire, sample/image/lab measures, attendance/non-attendance, result category, barriers
Vitals/tests/imaging timing: screening test first; diagnostic test after positive/abnormal; Japan collects BP/lipids/urinalysis etc.; Korea sends results and confirmation checkup
Results/referral/follow-up ownership: screening programme/navigator/specialist nurse; GP may receive info; diagnostic follow-up must be tracked
If symptoms continue but tests are norma: negative screen does not rule out symptomatic disease; repeat screening interval; primary-care presentation if symptoms
Signal-loss / black zones: non-return/no-show; abnormal result without diagnostic completion; false reassurance; mail/result comprehension

Regions/examples: Brazil FHS; India ASHA/HWC; iCCM/RAcE sub-Saharan Africa
Real entry points: household visit, community outreach, CHW/caregiver contact, population empanelment
First information collected: family registry, living conditions, symptoms, medication adherence, danger signs, screening eligibility/results
Vitals/tests/imaging timing: basic assessment at home; NCD/cancer screening at HWC; CHW danger-sign classification and referral
Results/referral/follow-up ownership: CHW relays to nurse/physician/CHO or referral facility; two-way referral/counter-referral varies
If symptoms continue but tests are norma: repeat home visit, adherence support, referral chase, community registry monitoring
Signal-loss / black zones: CHW notes not integrated; referral loop fails; supplies/training gaps; household risks not visible to clinician
